items.title: Broken object level authorization (BOLA/IDOR)
items.description: Swap object IDs across users and roles; test horizontal and vertical access.
items.how_to_find:
Authenticate as user A, capture a request referencing an object you own, then replay it as user B swapping the ID:

```
curl -s https://api.target/v1/orders/1001 -H "Authorization: Bearer $USER_B"
```

If B receives A's object, it's BOLA. Use Burp/Autorize to auto-compare responses across two sessions while iterating IDs.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce object-level authorization on every request server-side: verify the authenticated principal owns/has access to the referenced object before returning it. Use unpredictable IDs (UUIDv4) as defense-in-depth, and add tests asserting cross-user access returns `403/404`.

items.title: JWT validation flaws
items.description: alg:none, weak secrets, missing exp/aud, and key confusion attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect and attack the token with `jwt_tool`:

```
jwt_tool <token> -T
jwt_tool <token> -X a        # alg:none
jwt_tool <token> -X k -pk public.pem   # RS256->HS256 key confusion
```

Accepted `alg:none`, unverified signatures, or missing `exp`/`aud` checks are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Pin the expected algorithm (never accept `none`), verify signatures against the correct key type, and validate `exp`, `nbf`, `iss`, and `aud`. Use a vetted library, reject tokens failing any claim, and keep keys in a JWKS with rotation.

items.title: OAuth misconfiguration
items.description: Redirect URI validation, state parameter, and scope escalation.
items.how_to_find:
Test the flows: tamper `redirect_uri`, check `state` (CSRF), PKCE presence, and scope escalation:

```
GET /authorize?client_id=<c>&redirect_uri=https://evil.tld&response_type=code&state=x
```

Open redirects on `redirect_uri`, missing `state`/PKCE, or implicit-flow tokens in URLs are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Use authorization-code + PKCE, exact-match registered `redirect_uri`, enforce `state`, short-lived codes/tokens, and least-privilege scopes with consent. Disable the implicit flow. Validate `aud`/`azp` on resource servers.

items.title: API key exposure
items.description: Keys not in client bundles, logs, or referrer headers.
items.how_to_find:
Hunt for keys in client bundles, repos, and history:

```
gitleaks detect --source . --redact
trufflehog filesystem ./ --only-verified
grep -REo '(AKIA|sk_live_|AIza)[A-Za-z0-9_-]+' dist/
```
items.how_to_fix: Move keys server-side, never ship them in web/mobile clients, scope them minimally, and rotate on exposure. Add secret scanning to CI (gitleaks/trufflehog) as a blocking gate and store secrets in a manager.

items.title: Mass assignment
items.description: Extra JSON fields like role:admin accepted by backend.
items.how_to_find:
Send extra properties the client shouldn't control and see if they persist:

```
curl -s -X PATCH https://api.target/v1/users/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"email":"a@b.c","role":"admin","is_verified":true}'
```

If `role`/`is_verified` change, it's mass assignment.
items.how_to_fix: Bind only an explicit allowlist of writable fields (DTOs), ignore/reject unknown or server-controlled properties, and authorize sensitive fields separately. Add tests asserting privileged fields can't be set via the API.

items.title: Rate limiting and brute force
items.description: OTP, login, and password reset endpoints throttled.
items.how_to_find:
Fire many requests at auth/OTP/reset endpoints and watch for throttling:

```
ffuf -w passwords.txt -X POST -u https://api.target/login -d 'user=admin&pass=FUZZ' -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' -mc all
```

No `429`/lockout after many attempts = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Apply per-account and per-IP rate limits with lockout/backoff on auth, OTP, and reset endpoints; add CAPTCHA/step-up after thresholds. Enforce at the gateway and app, and alert on spikes. Verify `429` engages.

items.title: GraphQL introspection and depth limits
items.description: Disable introspection in prod; limit query depth and cost.
items.how_to_find:
Check introspection and try a deeply-nested query:

```
curl -s https://api.target/graphql -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"query":"{__schema{types{name}}}"}'
```

A full schema response (and no depth/complexity limit) is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable introspection in production, enforce query depth + cost/complexity limits, add timeouts and pagination caps, and prefer persisted-query allowlists. Verify introspection returns an error and deep queries are rejected.

items.title: Version sprawl
items.description: Old /api/v1 endpoints not left live with weaker auth.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate old/parallel versions still reachable:

```
ffuf -w <(printf 'v1\nv2\nv3\nbeta\ninternal\n') -u https://api.target/FUZZ/users -mc 200,401,403
```

Deprecated versions with weaker controls are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an inventory of API versions, retire deprecated ones on a published schedule, and route all versions through the same authz/rate-limit controls. Return `410 Gone` for retired versions.

items.title: Error message leakage
items.description: Stack traces and internal IDs not returned to clients.
items.how_to_find:
Trigger errors and inspect responses for stack traces, SQL, framework, or path disclosure:

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/users?id='" -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```
items.how_to_fix: Return generic error messages with a correlation ID; log details server-side only. Disable debug mode in production and add a global exception handler that scrubs sensitive detail.

sections.title: API Access Control — Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)

items.title: Tamper with sequential object identifiers
items.description: Increment, decrement, or substitute numeric object IDs in path and query parameters to access resources belonging to other users.
items.how_to_find:
Iterate numeric IDs with your own token and diff responses:

```
for id in $(seq 1000 1100); do echo -n "$id "; curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.target/v1/invoices/$id -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"; done
```

`200` on objects you don't own = critical BOLA.
items.how_to_fix: Authorize every object access against the caller's identity server-side; switch to non-guessable UUIDs. Add automated cross-user access tests (Autorize) to CI.

items.title: Access nested/child objects without ownership checks
items.description: Test child resources (e.g. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find:
Test child resources under a parent you don't own:

```
curl -s https://api.target/v1/accounts/999/transactions -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```

Access without an ownership check on the parent chain is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate ownership at every level of the resource hierarchy (parent and child), not just the leaf. Scope queries by the authenticated principal (e.g. `WHERE account.owner = :me`).

items.title: Probe batch / bulk endpoints for object leakage
items.description: Submit arrays of IDs to batch endpoints to see whether per-object authorization is skipped for items the caller does not own.
items.how_to_find:
Request multiple IDs in a batch/bulk call, including IDs you don't own:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/items:batchGet -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"ids":[1,2,3,9998,9999]}'
```

Returned foreign objects = finding (per-item authz missing).
items.how_to_fix: Apply per-item authorization inside batch handlers and filter out unauthorized IDs (return per-item errors, not data). Cap batch size and log denied items.

items.title: Assess predictability of UUID/GUID object references
items.description: Determine whether identifiers are guessable (sequential UUIDv1, timestamps, short tokens) so BOLA is exploitable without leaked IDs.
items.how_to_find:
Collect several IDs and check for predictability (sequential UUIDv1/timestamps, short random):

```
python3 -c "import uuid;print(uuid.UUID('<id>').version)"
```
items.how_to_fix: Use UUIDv4 / 128-bit random identifiers for externally-referenced objects; never expose sequential DB keys. Predictable IDs still require server-side authz (they are not an access control).

items.title: Test BOLA on PATCH and DELETE operations
items.description: Verify state-changing verbs re-check ownership; read access may be guarded while modify/delete on the same object is not.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt state changes on others' objects, not just reads:

```
curl -s -X DELETE https://api.target/v1/posts/4321 -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n'
curl -s -X PATCH https://api.target/v1/posts/4321 -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"title":"pwn"}'
```

Success on another user's object is critical.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce ownership checks on all mutating verbs (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE), not just GET. Add write-path cross-user tests.

items.title: Move object identifiers between path, query, and body
items.description: Relocate the object id into the request body or headers to bypass authorization logic that only inspects the URL path.
items.how_to_find:
Relocate the ID to bypass inconsistent checks:

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/orders?id=1001" -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/orders/get -d '{"id":1001}' -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```

If one location skips authz, it's a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Resolve and authorize the object identifier in a single, consistent server-side path regardless of where it arrives. Centralize authorization in middleware so every entry point is covered.

sections.title: API Access Control — Broken Function Level Authorization (BFLA)

items.title: Invoke administrative functions as a low-privilege user
items.description: Call admin-only endpoints (user management, config, reporting) with a standard user token to detect missing role enforcement.
items.how_to_find:
Capture admin endpoints (from JS/docs) and call them with a normal user token:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/admin/users/promote -H "Authorization: Bearer $LOW" -d '{"user":"me"}'
```

Success = critical BFLA.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce role/permission checks on every privileged function server-side (deny by default). Separate admin routes, require explicit roles, and add tests that low-privilege tokens get `403` on admin actions.

items.title: Bypass authorization via alternate HTTP methods
items.description: Switch GET to POST/PUT/DELETE (or use overrides) to reach privileged behavior guarded only for the documented method.
items.how_to_find:
Try methods that may skip checks:

```
curl -s -X PUT https://api.target/v1/admin/config -H "Authorization: Bearer $LOW"
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/admin/config -H 'X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE' -H "Authorization: Bearer $LOW"
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply authorization uniformly across all methods, reject unexpected verbs (`405`), and disable method-override headers unless required. Test each method for the same authz.

items.title: Enumerate endpoints lacking per-endpoint role checks
items.description: Map every route and confirm role/scope authorization is applied individually rather than relying on UI hiding or gateway-only rules.
items.how_to_find:
Diff accessible endpoints between admin and normal users (Burp/Autorize) and flag those returning data to low-priv tokens:

```
ffuf -w endpoints.txt -u https://api.target/FUZZ -H "Authorization: Bearer $LOW" -mc 200
```
items.how_to_fix: Adopt centralized, deny-by-default authorization middleware requiring an explicit permission per route. Fail closed for unmapped routes and cover with tests.

items.title: Discover hidden or guessable administrative paths
items.description: Brute-force admin namespaces (/admin, /internal, /v2/admin) that are unlinked but reachable without elevated authorization.
items.how_to_find:
Brute-force admin paths and inspect client code/sitemaps:

```
ffuf -w admin-wordlist.txt -u https://api.target/FUZZ -mc 200,401,403
```

Reachable `/admin`, `/internal`, `/debug` = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Don't rely on obscurity: require auth+authz on every admin route, remove/relocate internal endpoints off public hosts, and monitor access.

items.title: Tamper with role/scope claims in tokens or parameters
items.description: Modify role fields in JWT claims, cookies, or request parameters to escalate to privileged function access.
items.how_to_find:
Modify role in the token or request and see if it's trusted:

```
jwt_tool <token> -T   # edit "role":"admin", re-sign if key known
curl -s https://api.target/v1/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -H 'X-Role: admin'
```
items.how_to_fix: Derive authorization from server-side session/claims validated against a trusted store, never from client-supplied headers/params. Verify token signatures and ignore role hints from the client.

items.title: Cross-tenant privileged function access
items.description: Attempt privileged operations scoped to another tenant/organization to verify function authorization is tenant-aware.
items.how_to_find:
As a tenant-A admin, invoke functions scoped to tenant B:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/tenants/B/settings -H "Authorization: Bearer $TENANT_A_ADMIN"
```

Success = critical tenant isolation break.
items.how_to_fix: Bind every request to the caller's tenant and enforce `tenant_id` matches on all queries/functions. Add cross-tenant negative tests and tenant-scoped DB row-level security.

sections.title: API Access Control — Broken Object Property Level Authorization

items.title: Inject privileged properties via mass assignment
items.description: Add unexpected fields (isAdmin, role, balance, verified) to create/update bodies to test for blind binding to the data model.
items.how_to_find:
Add server-controlled fields to create/update bodies:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/users -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"name":"x","role":"admin","account_balance":99999}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Use explicit write allowlists (DTOs) and strip unknown/privileged fields before persistence. Authorize sensitive properties separately.

items.title: Detect excessive data exposure in responses
items.description: Inspect responses for sensitive properties (PII, hashes, internal flags) the client should never receive but the API returns wholesale.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect responses for fields the client shouldn't see (password hashes, internal flags, PII):

```
curl -s https://api.target/v1/users/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" | jq
```
items.how_to_fix: Serialize responses via explicit output schemas/DTOs (allowlist), not the raw model. Filter server-side, never rely on the client to hide fields.

items.title: Write to read-only or server-controlled properties
items.description: Attempt to modify fields meant to be immutable (created_at, owner_id, status) to verify property-level write authorization.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt to set fields like `created_at`, `id`, `owner`, `verified`:

```
curl -s -X PATCH https://api.target/v1/docs/10 -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"owner":"me","created_at":"2000-01-01"}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Mark server-controlled fields immutable in the API layer; reject or ignore attempts to write them and log the attempt. Enforce with DTO validation.

items.title: Bypass response field filtering
items.description: Use field-selection or include parameters (fields=, expand=, ?include) to coerce the API into returning restricted properties.
items.how_to_find:
Use field-selection/`?fields=`/GraphQL to pull normally-hidden properties:

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/users/me?fields=*" -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```

If filtering is client-side or bypassable, it's critical.
items.how_to_fix: Apply property-level authorization on the server for every field regardless of selection params. Never return unauthorized fields even when explicitly requested.

items.title: Set nested or relational privileged properties
items.description: Inject nested objects (user.roles, account.owner) in payloads to exploit deep mass-assignment paths.
items.how_to_find:
Try nested objects to set privileged relations:

```
curl -s -X PATCH https://api.target/v1/users/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"profile":{"role":{"name":"admin"}}}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Validate and authorize nested/relational writes with the same allowlist rules as top-level fields; don't deep-merge untrusted input into entities.

items.title: Surface debug/internal properties in error responses
items.description: Trigger errors and edge cases to reveal internal property names or values that leak through verbose serialization.
items.how_to_find:
Trigger errors and look for internal fields (SQL, object dumps, internal IDs):

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/items -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"qty":"NaN"}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Return sanitized errors without internal object state; disable debug serializers in production and scrub fields in a global error handler.

items.title: Test weak or missing token validation
items.description: Submit malformed, expired, none-algorithm, or tampered tokens to confirm the server rigorously validates signature, expiry, and audience.
items.how_to_find:
Send tampered/expired/none tokens and unauthenticated requests:

```
curl -s https://api.target/v1/me -H "Authorization: Bearer expired.or.tampered"
curl -s https://api.target/v1/me    # no token
```

`200` for invalid/absent tokens is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Verify signature, expiry, issuer, and audience on every request; reject malformed/expired tokens with `401`. Use vetted middleware and fail closed.

items.title: Find endpoints missing authentication
items.description: Replay requests without credentials and brute-force routes to locate endpoints that respond without any authentication.
items.how_to_find:
Crawl the API and hit each route with no token:

```
ffuf -w endpoints.txt -u https://api.target/FUZZ -mc 200
```

Sensitive data returned without auth = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require authentication by default (deny-by-default gateway/middleware); explicitly allowlist public routes. Add tests asserting protected routes return `401` unauthenticated.

items.title: Assess credential stuffing and brute-force protections
items.description: Verify login and token endpoints enforce rate limiting, lockout, and anomaly detection against automated credential attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Automate login attempts with known combos and observe throttling/lockout:

```
ffuf -w combos.txt -X POST -u https://api.target/login -d 'email=FUZZ1&password=FUZZ2' -mode clusterbomb -mc all
```
items.how_to_fix: Add per-account+IP rate limiting, lockout/backoff, CAPTCHA/step-up, breached-password checks, and MFA. Monitor and alert on distributed attempts. Verify throttling engages.

items.title: Exploit JWT implementation flaws
items.description: Pointer to JWT-specific attacks: alg confusion, none algorithm, weak HMAC secrets, kid injection, and unverified jku/x5u.
items.how_to_find:
Run the JWT attack suite:

```
jwt_tool <token> -M at -t https://api.target/v1/me -rh "Authorization: Bearer <token>"
jwt_tool <token> -X a   # none
jwt_tool <token> -X k -pk public.pem   # RS256->HS256 confusion
```
items.how_to_fix: Pin algorithms, verify signatures with the correct key, validate all claims, keep short expiry, and rotate keys via JWKS. Never accept `none` or client-selected algorithms.

items.title: Review the JWKS endpoint and key hygiene
items.description: The public JWKS (/.well-known/jwks.json) governs which keys verify tokens; stale, duplicate, or attacker-influencable keys widen the forgery surface and complicate revocation.
items.how_to_find:
Fetch JWKS and check keys:

```
curl -s https://api.target/.well-known/jwks.json | jq '.keys[] | {kid,alg,use}'
```

Stale keys, weak keys, or a spoofable `jku`/`x5u` are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Serve JWKS over TLS, rotate keys with overlapping `kid`s, remove retired keys, ignore token-supplied `jku`/`x5u`, and use strong keys (RSA>=2048 / EC P-256). Cache with sane TTLs.

items.title: Detect API keys or tokens passed in the URL
items.description: Identify credentials sent as query parameters that leak via logs, referrer headers, browser history, and proxies.
items.how_to_find:
Look for credentials in query strings (logged everywhere):

```
grep -RiE '[?&](api_?key|token|access_token)=' access.log
```

Any secret in URL = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Move credentials to the `Authorization` header or a POST body; reject query-string tokens. Scrub logs and rotate any exposed keys.

items.title: Test password reset and recovery flows
items.description: Probe reset-token entropy, reuse, expiry, and host-header poisoning that allow account takeover via the recovery API.
items.how_to_find:
Probe reset for token predictability, no expiry, host-header poisoning, and user enumeration:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/reset -d '{"email":"victim@x.com"}' -H 'Host: evil.tld'
```
items.how_to_fix: Use single-use, short-lived, high-entropy reset tokens tied to the account; don't leak existence (uniform responses); ignore untrusted `Host`/forwarded headers when building links; rate-limit and log resets.

items.title: Check for keys exposed in URLs and logs
items.description: Confirm keys are not transmitted in query strings or written to access logs, error logs, or analytics pipelines.
items.how_to_find:
Search logs, referrers, and history for keys in URLs:

```
grep -RiE '[?&](key|apikey|token)=' /var/log/ 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Never accept credentials in URLs; use headers. Scrub existing logs, rotate exposed keys, and add detection for URL-borne secrets.

items.title: Verify key and token rotation support
items.description: Confirm the platform supports and enforces periodic rotation and revocation of keys and long-lived tokens.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether keys/tokens can be rotated without downtime (overlapping validity, per-client keys):

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/keys:rotate -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN"
```

No rotation mechanism = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Support overlapping key versions, per-client keys, and self-service rotation with revocation. Automate periodic rotation and rotate immediately on suspected compromise.

items.title: Assess over-scoped key permissions
items.description: Verify keys follow least privilege; a read-only key should not perform writes or reach unrelated services.
items.how_to_find:
Test what a given key can do beyond its purpose:

```
curl -s https://api.target/v1/admin/users -H "X-API-Key: $READONLY_KEY"
```

A "read-only" key performing admin/writes = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Scope keys to least privilege (specific endpoints/actions), separate read vs write, and bind to source IPs where feasible. Review scopes regularly.

items.title: Test for missing token/key expiry
items.description: Confirm tokens and keys carry sensible lifetimes and that expired credentials are rejected.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether tokens/keys expire:

```
jwt_tool <token> -T | grep -i exp
```

No `exp` (or non-expiring API keys) = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Set short expiry on access tokens with refresh rotation; give API keys an expiry/rotation policy and support revocation. Reject tokens lacking `exp`.

items.title: Hunt for keys leaked in client-side code
items.description: Search SPA bundles, mobile apps, and public repositories for embedded keys or secrets usable directly against the API.
items.how_to_find:
Scan web/mobile bundles:

```
gitleaks detect --source . --redact
apkleaks -f app.apk
```
items.how_to_fix: Keep secrets server-side; use a backend-for-frontend so clients never hold long-lived keys. Rotate leaked keys and add client-bundle secret scanning to CI.

items.title: Check token binding and reuse controls
items.description: Verify tokens are bound to client/IP/origin where appropriate and cannot be replayed from arbitrary contexts.
items.how_to_find:
Replay a token from a different IP/device/client and check for reuse detection:

```
curl -s https://api.target/v1/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $STOLEN" --interface <other-ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Bind tokens to client/device where possible (DPoP/mTLS), detect refresh-token reuse (rotate + revoke family on replay), and keep access tokens short-lived. Log anomalous reuse.

items.title: HTTP verb tampering
items.description: Send unexpected verbs (PUT, DELETE, PATCH, TRACE) to endpoints to reach behavior the application does not intend to expose.
items.how_to_find:
Swap verbs to reach unintended handlers or bypass authz:

```
curl -s -X PUT https://api.target/v1/users/1 -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
curl -s -X HEAD https://api.target/v1/admin -H "Authorization: Bearer $LOW"
```
items.how_to_fix: Map methods explicitly per route, return `405` for unsupported verbs, and apply the same authorization to all methods. Don't rely on framework auto-routing.

items.title: Content-type confusion
items.description: Swap Content-Type (JSON vs form vs XML) to bypass validation, trigger different parsers, or reach mass-assignment paths.
items.how_to_find:
Change `Content-Type` to bypass parsers/validation (e.g. JSON vs form vs XML for XXE):

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/items -H 'Content-Type: application/xml' --data '<x>..</x>'
```
items.how_to_fix: Accept only expected content types (reject others with `415`), disable dangerous parsers (XXE-safe XML), and validate after parsing. Don't switch security behavior on content type.

items.title: HTTP method override headers
items.description: Abuse X-HTTP-Method-Override / _method to convert allowed verbs into restricted ones at the framework layer.
items.how_to_find:
Try override headers to smuggle methods past proxies/WAFs:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/orders/1 -H 'X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE' -H "Authorization: Bearer $LOW"
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable method-override headers unless explicitly needed; if required, authorize the effective method. Ensure WAF/gateway and app agree on the method.

items.title: Pagination and limit abuse
items.description: Manipulate page size, offset, and cursor parameters to extract bulk data or cause expensive enumeration.
items.how_to_find:
Request huge/negative page sizes to exhaust resources:

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/items?limit=1000000&offset=-1" -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce max page size, validate `limit`/`offset` bounds, use cursor pagination, and add query timeouts. Reject out-of-range values with `400`.

items.title: Information leakage via status codes
items.description: Compare 401/403/404/200 responses across resources to infer existence of objects, users, or endpoints.
items.how_to_find:
Compare responses for existing vs non-existing objects/users (oracle):

```
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.target/v1/users/known
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.target/v1/users/unknown
```

Distinct codes/timing revealing existence = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Return uniform responses (`404`/generic) for authz failures and unknown vs unauthorized objects; avoid timing oracles. Prefer `404` over `403` where existence itself is sensitive.

items.title: CORS misconfiguration for APIs
items.description: Test reflected origins, null origin, and wildcard-with-credentials configurations that allow cross-origin data theft.
items.how_to_find:
Test reflected origins + credentials:

```
curl -s -I https://api.target/v1/me -H 'Origin: https://evil.tld' | grep -i access-control
```

`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.tld` with `Allow-Credentials: true` (or `*` with creds) is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist exact trusted origins, never reflect arbitrary `Origin`, and never combine `*` with credentials. Restrict methods/headers and validate on the server.

items.title: Injection through REST parameters
items.description: Fuzz JSON/query/path parameters for SQL, NoSQL, command, and template injection in REST handlers.
items.how_to_find:
Fuzz params for SQLi/NoSQLi/command/template injection:

```
sqlmap -u "https://api.target/v1/search?q=1" --headers="Authorization: Bearer $T" --batch
curl -s https://api.target/v1/find -d '{"user":{"$ne":null}}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
```
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries / safe ODM operators, validate/allowlist inputs, and avoid passing user data to shells/templates. Add WAF + tests for injection classes.

items.title: Introspection enabled in production
items.description: Query the __schema introspection system to map all types, queries, and mutations exposed by the endpoint.
items.how_to_find:
Query the schema:

```
curl -s https://api.target/graphql -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"query":"{__schema{queryType{name} types{name}}}"}'
```

Full schema returned in prod = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable introspection in production (or restrict to authenticated admins). Ship the schema to trusted clients out-of-band. Verify introspection is rejected.

items.title: Deeply-nested query denial of service
items.description: Construct recursively nested queries via circular relationships to exhaust CPU/memory on the resolver layer.
items.how_to_find:
Send a recursive nested query on a cyclic relation:

```
curl -s https://api.target/graphql -d '{"query":"{a{b{a{b{a{b{a{b{id}}}}}}}}}"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
```

High latency / resource spike = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a max query depth and cost/complexity budget, add timeouts, and cap list sizes. Reject queries exceeding limits with a clear error.

items.title: Batching and alias-based abuse
items.description: Use query batching and field aliases to multiply operations in one request, bypassing rate limits and amplifying brute force.
items.how_to_find:
Use aliases/batching to multiply work (e.g. brute force via one request):

```
{"query":"{a1:login(u:\"x\",p:\"1\"){t} a2:login(u:\"x\",p:\"2\"){t}}"}
```
items.how_to_fix: Limit aliases/operations per request, disable array-batching or cap it, and apply rate limits by resolved cost (not request count). Treat each aliased field as a counted operation.

items.title: Field suggestion / schema leakage
items.description: Send near-miss field names to harvest "did you mean" suggestions that reveal schema even when introspection is disabled.
items.how_to_find:
Send typo'd fields; servers often suggest valid names (leaks schema even with introspection off):

```
curl -s https://api.target/graphql -d '{"query":"{usr{id}}"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
```

"Did you mean user?" = leakage.
items.how_to_fix: Disable field suggestions in production (framework setting), return generic validation errors, and keep introspection off. Validate against a persisted-query allowlist.

items.title: Injection via GraphQL arguments
items.description: Pass SQL/NoSQL/OS payloads through query and mutation arguments to reach injection sinks behind resolvers.
items.how_to_find:
Inject into resolver arguments reaching a datastore/shell:

```
{"query":"{users(filter:\"1 OR 1=1\"){id}}"}
```
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries in resolvers, validate/type argument inputs, and never concatenate arguments into queries/commands. Add injection tests per resolver.

items.title: Per-resolver authorization bypass
items.description: Verify each field/resolver enforces authorization rather than relying on a single top-level check.
items.how_to_find:
Reach sensitive fields through alternate paths/relations that skip authz:

```
{"query":"{me{friends{privateData{ssn}}}}"}
```

Data returned without per-resolver authz = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authorization in each resolver (or via a directive/middleware) on the object being resolved, including nested/relational fields. Don't authorize only at the top query.

items.title: CSRF against GraphQL endpoints
items.description: Test whether GET-based or form-encoded GraphQL requests allow cross-site state-changing operations without CSRF protection.
items.how_to_find:
If the endpoint accepts GET or form-encoded POST with cookies, test CSRF:

```
curl -s 'https://api.target/graphql?query={me{id}}' -b 'session=<victim>'
```
items.how_to_fix: Require `application/json` + a CSRF token or non-cookie auth (bearer), reject GET for mutations, and validate `Origin`/`Sec-Fetch`. Disable form-encoded GraphQL.

items.title: Mutation abuse and unauthorized state change
items.description: Enumerate mutations and attempt privileged or unintended state changes available to low-privilege users.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate mutations and invoke privileged ones as a low-priv user:

```
{"query":"mutation{promoteUser(id:\"me\",role:ADMIN){id role}}"}
```
items.how_to_fix: Authorize every mutation against the caller and target, validate inputs, and rate-limit state-changing operations. Add negative tests for privileged mutations.

items.title: Missing query cost / complexity limits
items.description: Verify the server enforces query depth, complexity, and cost analysis to prevent expensive query DoS.
items.how_to_find:
Send an expensive multi-field/list query and measure cost:

```
{"query":"{allUsers(first:100000){edges{node{orders{items{product{reviews{comments{id}}}}}}}}}"}
```
items.how_to_fix: Assign field costs and enforce a per-request complexity budget (graphql-cost-analysis / envelope plugins), plus timeouts and pagination caps. Reject over-budget queries.

items.title: Persisted-query allowlist bypass
items.description: Test whether ad-hoc queries are accepted when only persisted/allowlisted operations should be, or APQ hash handling can be abused.
items.how_to_find:
If using persisted queries, test whether arbitrary queries are still accepted:

```
curl -s https://api.target/graphql -d '{"query":"{__typename}"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
```

Ad-hoc queries accepted despite an allowlist = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce the persisted-query allowlist strictly in production (reject non-persisted operations), verify the query hash server-side, and manage the allowlist via CI.

sections.title: API Resource & Posture — Unrestricted Resource Consumption

items.title: Missing or weak rate limiting
items.description: Send high-volume requests to confirm per-user, per-IP, and per-endpoint rate limits exist and are enforced.
items.how_to_find:
Blast an endpoint and watch for `429`:

```
seq 1 500 | xargs -P50 -I{} curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.target/v1/search -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" | sort | uniq -c
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce per-user/per-IP/global rate limits at the gateway and app, with quotas and `429 + Retry-After`. Tune by endpoint cost and alert on abuse.

items.title: Oversized request payloads
items.description: Submit very large bodies, deeply nested JSON, or many fields to test body-size and parser limits.
items.how_to_find:
Send a huge body to test size caps:

```
head -c 100M /dev/zero | curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/upload --data-binary @- -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n'
```
items.how_to_fix: Set max body size at the proxy and app (reject with `413`), stream large uploads, and validate `Content-Length`. Apply per-field size limits.

items.title: Expensive operation abuse
items.description: Trigger costly operations (exports, reports, image processing, search) repeatedly to assess resource controls.
items.how_to_find:
Hammer costly endpoints (reports, exports, search, image processing):

```
seq 1 50 | xargs -P20 -I{} curl -s -o /dev/null https://api.target/v1/reports/export -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```
items.how_to_fix: Rate-limit/queue expensive operations, run them async with quotas, add timeouts + circuit breakers, and cache results. Charge cost against the caller's quota.

items.title: No pagination caps
items.description: Request unbounded result sets via limit/page-size parameters to confirm maximum result caps are enforced.
items.how_to_find:
Request an unbounded list:

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/items?limit=100000000" -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -o /dev/null -w '%{size_download} bytes\n'
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a hard max page size, default sensible limits, and cursor pagination with query timeouts. Reject oversized `limit` with `400`.

items.title: File upload and multipart amplification
items.description: Upload large or many files (zip bombs, huge media) to test upload size limits and processing safeguards.
items.how_to_find:
Upload many/large parts or a decompression bomb:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/upload -F 'f=@bomb.zip' -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```
items.how_to_fix: Cap file count/size/parts, validate content types, scan uploads, and guard against decompression bombs (limit output size). Process async with quotas.

items.title: Amplified third-party / billing cost abuse
items.description: Identify endpoints that trigger paid downstream calls (SMS, email, LLM) lacking quotas, causing financial denial of service.
items.how_to_find:
Find endpoints that trigger paid downstream calls (SMS, email, LLM, cloud) and invoke repeatedly:

```
seq 1 100 | xargs -P20 -I{} curl -s -o /dev/null -X POST https://api.target/v1/notify -d '{"phone":"+1..."}' -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```
items.how_to_fix: Rate-limit and quota per-user on cost-incurring endpoints, add spend caps/alerts on downstream providers, require verification for high-cost actions, and detect anomalies.

items.title: Rate-limit bypass via spoofed client headers
items.description: Per-IP throttles keyed on a forwarding header rather than the real connection can be reset by rotating the header value, defeating the limit entirely.
items.how_to_find:
Rotate `X-Forwarded-For`/`X-Real-IP` to evade IP-based limits:

```
for i in $(seq 1 200); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.target/login -H "X-Forwarded-For: 10.0.0.$((RANDOM%255))" -d 'u=a&p=b'; done
```
items.how_to_fix: Rate-limit on authenticated identity (not just client IP), only trust `X-Forwarded-For` from your own proxies (fixed hop count), and combine identity + IP + device signals. Verify spoofed headers don't reset counters.

sections.title: API Resource & Posture — Server-Side Request Forgery

items.title: URL parameters fetched server-side
items.description: Identify parameters (url, src, image, callback) the server requests and point them at internal or attacker-controlled hosts.
items.how_to_find:
Find params that cause the server to fetch a URL, then point them internally:

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/" -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:6379/" -H "Authorization: Bearer $T"
```
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist destination hosts/schemes, resolve+validate the IP (block private/link-local/loopback) after DNS resolution, disable redirects, and fetch via an egress proxy. Never pass raw user URLs to the fetcher.

items.title: Webhook and callback URL abuse
items.description: Register webhook/callback URLs pointing at internal services to coerce server-side requests to private resources.
items.how_to_find:
Register a webhook/callback pointing at internal resources:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/webhooks -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"url":"http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"}'
```

Server-side requests to internal targets = critical SSRF.
items.how_to_fix: Validate webhook URLs against an allowlist, block internal/link-local ranges post-resolution, require domain verification, send from a locked-down egress with no metadata access, and sign outbound requests.

items.title: Cloud metadata endpoint access
items.description: Target 169.254.169.254 (and equivalents) via SSRF to retrieve cloud instance credentials and metadata.
items.how_to_find:
Via an SSRF sink, reach the metadata service for credentials:

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/"
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce IMDSv2 with hop-limit 1, block `169.254.169.254` at the egress firewall/host, and use workload identity (IRSA/Workload Identity) with minimal scope. Validate/allowlist SSRF sinks.

items.title: DNS rebinding to bypass host checks
items.description: Use TOCTOU DNS rebinding so a validated hostname resolves to an internal IP at fetch time.
items.how_to_find:
Use a domain whose DNS flips to an internal IP after the allowlist check (TOCTOU):

```
curl -s "https://api.target/v1/fetch?url=http://rebind.attacker.tld/"
```
items.how_to_fix: Resolve the hostname once, validate the resolved IP, and connect to that same pinned IP — don't re-resolve. Block private ranges and disable redirects. Use an egress proxy that enforces the pinned IP.

items.title: SSRF allowlist/blocklist bypass
items.description: Bypass URL filters using IP encodings, redirects, alternate schemes, IPv6, and decimal/hex representations.
items.how_to_find:
Try encodings/alternate representations to evade filters:

```
http://0x7f000001/    http://2130706433/    http://127.0.0.1.nip.io/    http://[::1]/    http://127.1/
```
items.how_to_fix: Prefer allowlists over blocklists; normalize and resolve to an IP, then check the numeric IP against denied ranges (v4+v6; decimal/hex/octal covered). Disable redirects and unusual schemes.

items.title: Blind SSRF via out-of-band detection
items.description: Inject collaborator/OAST URLs to detect blind SSRF where no response content is returned.
items.how_to_find:
When no response is reflected, use an OOB canary:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/import -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"url":"http://<id>.oastify.com/"}'
# watch Burp Collaborator / interactsh for DNS+HTTP callbacks
```
items.how_to_fix: Same controls as SSRF (allowlist, IP validation, no redirects, locked egress). Additionally monitor egress DNS/HTTP for anomalies and remove unnecessary outbound access from app servers.

sections.title: API Resource & Posture — Improper Inventory Management

items.title: Old or deprecated API versions reachable
items.description: Probe /v1, /v0, and legacy paths that may lack current security controls but remain live.
items.how_to_find:
Probe historical versions:

```
ffuf -w <(printf 'v1\nv2\nv0\nlegacy\n') -u https://api.target/FUZZ/users -mc 200,401,403
```
items.how_to_fix: Inventory all versions, retire deprecated ones (return `410`), and route every live version through current authz/rate-limit controls. Publish a deprecation schedule.

items.title: Undocumented / shadow APIs
items.description: Discover endpoints absent from documentation via brute force, JS analysis, and traffic inspection.
items.how_to_find:
Discover endpoints not in the spec via crawling, JS analysis, and traffic capture:

```
katana -u https://app.target -jc | grep -Eo '/api/[^"]+' | sort -u
```

Compare against the OpenAPI spec; extras are shadow APIs.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an authoritative API inventory (auto-generated from a gateway/spec), route all traffic through the gateway, and detect/alert on undocumented routes. Decommission or document discovered endpoints.

items.title: Exposed non-production environments
items.description: Identify staging, dev, test, and debug hosts reachable from the internet that may hold weaker controls or real data.
items.how_to_find:
Look for staging/dev hosts with real data and weak controls:

```
ffuf -w <(printf 'dev
staging
test
uat
qa
') -u https://FUZZ.api.target -mc all
```
items.how_to_fix: Isolate non-prod behind VPN/allowlists, use synthetic data, and apply the same auth. Never expose non-prod publicly; monitor for accidental exposure.

items.title: Missing API version retirement
items.description: Verify a documented lifecycle exists so superseded versions are decommissioned rather than left running indefinitely.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether deprecated versions are actually turned off (not just marked deprecated):

```
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.target/v1/users   # expect 410 if retired
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce end-of-life: after the deprecation window, disable the version and return `410 Gone`. Track usage to migrate remaining clients first.

items.title: API host and subdomain sprawl
items.description: Enumerate API subdomains and gateways to find forgotten hosts that escape monitoring and patching.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate API-bearing subdomains:

```
subfinder -d target.com -silent | httpx -silent -path /api -mc 200,401,403
```
items.how_to_fix: Consolidate APIs behind a managed gateway, retire orphaned hosts, keep DNS inventory current, and continuously discover new subdomains. Decommission stale endpoints.

items.title: Documentation and spec drift
items.description: Compare OpenAPI/Swagger specs against live behavior to find endpoints/params present in one but not the other.
items.how_to_find:
Diff the live API against its OpenAPI spec (extra/missing/changed endpoints, params):

```
schemathesis run https://api.target/openapi.json --checks all
```
items.how_to_fix: Generate docs from code (or contract-test against the spec in CI), fail builds on drift, and version the spec. Keep a single source of truth for the API contract.

items.title: Missing security headers
items.description: Check for absent HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, and cache-control headers on API responses.
items.how_to_find:
Check response headers:

```
curl -s -I https://api.target/v1/health | grep -iE 'strict-transport|content-type-options|frame-options|content-security'
```

Missing HSTS / `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` etc. are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Set HSTS, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, appropriate `Cache-Control` for sensitive data, and a strict CSP where responses render. Apply centrally at the gateway.

items.title: Verbose error messages and stack traces
items.description: Trigger errors to reveal stack traces, framework versions, queries, or file paths leaking internal detail.
items.how_to_find:
Trigger 500s and malformed input; inspect for stack traces / framework/version disclosure:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/v1/items -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --data 'notjson'
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable debug mode in production, return generic errors with a correlation ID, and log detail server-side. Add a global exception handler.

items.title: Unnecessary HTTP methods enabled
items.description: Enumerate allowed methods (TRACE, OPTIONS, PUT, DELETE) and confirm dangerous or unused verbs are disabled.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate allowed methods:

```
curl -s -X OPTIONS https://api.target/v1/users -i | grep -i allow
```

`TRACE`/`PUT`/`DELETE` where unneeded are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Disable unused methods (return `405`), turn off `TRACE`, and restrict `OPTIONS` responses. Define allowed methods per route.

items.title: Default configurations and credentials
items.description: Identify default admin paths, sample data, debug endpoints, and default credentials left from setup.
items.how_to_find:
Test default creds/routes on API components (admin panels, management endpoints):

```
curl -s -u admin:admin https://api.target/actuator/env
```
items.how_to_fix: Change all defaults, disable/secure management endpoints (e.g. Spring `actuator`), remove sample apps, and harden per a config baseline. Scan for defaults in CI.

items.title: Weak transport security
items.description: Verify TLS configuration, certificate validity, and absence of cleartext API endpoints.
items.how_to_find:
Test TLS config:

```
testssl.sh https://api.target
```

TLS 1.0/1.1, weak ciphers, or no HSTS are critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require TLS 1.2+/1.3, disable weak ciphers/protocols, enable HSTS (preload), and use valid certs with automated renewal. Redirect HTTP->HTTPS. Re-scan with testssl.sh.

items.title: Overly permissive CORS policy
items.description: Check for wildcard origins or credentialed wildcard CORS that allows untrusted sites to call the API.
items.how_to_find:
Test origin reflection + credentials:

```
curl -s -I https://api.target/v1/me -H 'Origin: https://evil.tld' | grep -i access-control-allow
```

Reflected origin + `Allow-Credentials: true` (or `*` with creds) = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist exact origins, never reflect arbitrary `Origin`, never pair `*` with credentials, and limit methods/headers. Validate CORS server-side and test with unexpected origins.

sections.title: API Resource & Posture — Unsafe Consumption of APIs

items.title: Test for blind trust of third-party API data
items.description: Data from integrated APIs consumed without validation can carry injection or malformed payloads downstream.
items.how_to_find: Return malicious/oversized/malformed data from a mock third-party and observe how the consumer handles it (point the integration at a mock returning huge/negative/injection payloads). Crashes, injection, or trust of unvalidated data = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate and sanitize all data received from third parties as untrusted input; enforce schemas, size limits, and timeouts; fail safe on unexpected data. Never pass it unescaped into sinks.

items.title: Verify transport security to integrated services
items.description: Calls to upstream APIs over plaintext or with disabled cert validation expose data to interception.
items.how_to_find:
Check outbound calls use TLS with cert validation (not disabled):

```
grep -RniE 'verify\s*=\s*False|rejectUnauthorized:\s*false|InsecureSkipVerify' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use TLS 1.2+/1.3 with strict cert validation for all outbound integrations, pin where feasible, and store integration creds securely. Never disable verification.

items.title: Test handling of redirects from third-party APIs
items.description: Blindly following redirects returned by an integrated API can enable SSRF or data exfiltration.
items.how_to_find:
See if the consumer blindly follows redirects returned by an integrated API (to internal targets / SSRF):

```
# mock third-party responds 302 -> http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
```

Following such redirects = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable automatic redirect following for server-to-server calls (or validate the redirect target against an allowlist and block internal ranges). Cap redirect depth and log unexpected redirects.

items.title: Trace consumed data into injection sinks
items.description: Third-party data reaching SQL/command/template/HTML sinks is exploitable just like direct user input.
items.how_to_find:
Follow third-party data into DB queries, templates, shells, or HTML and test for injection:

```
grep -RniE 'exec|eval|render|query\(' src/ | grep -i 'third_party\|external\|response'
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat all consumed data as untrusted: parameterize queries, escape output, avoid dynamic eval/templating, and validate against schemas before use in any sink.

items.title: Test inbound webhook signature verification
items.description: Endpoints that receive provider webhooks (payment, VCS, CI) must verify the HMAC/asymmetric signature; missing or flawed verification lets an attacker forge state-changing events.
items.how_to_find:
Send a webhook with a missing/invalid signature and see if it's accepted:

```
curl -s -X POST https://api.target/webhooks/stripe -d '{"type":"payout","amount":99999}' -H 'Stripe-Signature: invalid'
```

Accepted without valid HMAC = finding (forged events).
items.how_to_fix: Verify the HMAC/signature on every inbound webhook against the shared secret, check timestamp/nonce to prevent replay, and reject unsigned/invalid requests. Restrict source IPs where the provider supports it.
